question: A factory owner insures his warehouse against fire. Six months later he sells the warehouse but keeps the policy running. It burns down the next week. Why is his claim likely to fail?
options: He no longer has insurable interest at the time of loss
The policy lapsed automatically on sale
explanation: General insurance requires insurable interest at BOTH inception and the time of loss. He had it at inception but lost it when he sold, so at the moment of the fire he had none.
reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, insurable interest timing (general)

question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement of an insurable risk?
options: The loss must be measurable in money
There must be a large number of similar exposure units
The loss must offer the chance of financial gain
explanation: A chance of gain makes a risk speculative and UNinsurable. Insurable risks are pure risks: measurable, numerous, fortuitous, not catastrophic to the insurer. Read 'NOT' carefully.
reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, requirements of insurable risk

question: An agent tells a client a minor detail is unimportant and not to bother disclosing it. The client relies on this. The doctrine that may stop the insurer denying the claim later is:
explanation: Waiver: the insurer, through its agent, gave up the right to that information, so it cannot later rely on non-disclosure of it. Indemnity/subrogation/contribution are unrelated claim-settlement principles.

question: SDIC's policy owners' protection scheme is designed to:
options: Protect policy owners if an insurer fails, within scheme limits
explanation: The Policy Owners' Protection Scheme (run by SDIC) protects policyholders if an insurer becomes insolvent, subject to caps. It doesn't guarantee returns or cover mis-selling.

question: The likelihood that a policyholder will act dishonestly, such as staging a loss, is called:
explanation: Moral hazard concerns dishonest conduct or intent. Physical hazard is a bodily/property characteristic; anti-selection is worse-than-average risks being keener to buy.

question: Which pair correctly matches the market body to its role?
options: LIA regulates insurers; MAS is an industry association
MAS is the regulator; LIA is the life insurers' industry association
FIDReC sets premium rates; SDIC mediates disputes
GIA regulates life insurance; LIA regulates general insurance
explanation: MAS is the statutory regulator; LIA is the Life Insurance Association (industry body). FIDReC resolves disputes, SDIC runs protection schemes, GIA is the general insurance association.

question: A man buys a life policy on his business partner, on whom the business depends financially. At what point must insurable interest exist?
options: At the time of the partner's death only
Insurable interest is not required between business partners
explanation: Life insurance requires insurable interest at inception only. A business partner with financial dependency is a recognised insurable-interest relationship.

question: The loading added to a net premium covers all EXCEPT:
options: An allowance for policies that lapse early
The mortality cost of the risk
explanation: Mortality cost is part of the NET premium, not the loading. Loading covers expenses, commission, lapse allowance and profit. This is your net-vs-gross distinction as a NOT-question.

question: A policy owner wants to pay for his whole life plan over just 15 years instead of for life, then stop. Compared with paying for life, each premium will be:
options: Lower, because fewer years of cover
Higher, because the same cost is compressed into fewer payments
The same, because cover is identical
Zero after year 15 with no effect on cost
explanation: Limited payment compresses the same lifetime cost into fewer instalments, so each premium is higher. Cover still runs for life.
reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 2, payment modes / Ch 4 limited payment

question: Which premium mode consists of a series of stand-alone single premiums, where stopping at any time leaves the policy fully paid-up rather than lapsed?
explanation: Recurrent single premium: each payment is its own single premium buying a paid-up chunk, so stopping never causes a lapse. A single premium is ONE payment only; regular premium lapses if unpaid.

question: Which factor would NOT normally affect the premium an insurer charges for a life policy?
options: The applicant's occupation as a librarian versus an accountant, both low-risk
explanation: Between two equally low-risk desk occupations, occupation makes no rating difference. Smoking, sum assured and payment frequency are all listed pricing factors.

question: An insurer assumes a 4% investment return when pricing. Actual returns come in at 2%. Who bears the shortfall on a guaranteed non-par policy?
options: The policyholder, via a higher premium next year
explanation: Premiums are priced on the assumed rate; the insurer carries the gap if actual returns fall short on guaranteed non-par business. Non-par has no bonus to cut.

question: For a whole life policy, a woman aged 30 is typically charged a premium based on the mortality of:
explanation: Women live longer, so 'reduction in age' prices a woman as a younger man for life cover. (Health insurance flips this above 65.)

question: Which statement about statutory insurance funds is correct?
options: All three fund types must be kept separate from each other
Participating and non-participating may share a fund; investment-linked must be separate
Investment-linked may share with participating; non-par must be separate
Funds may be freely combined at the insurer's discretion
explanation: Par and non-par MAY share a fund; ILP MUST be separate. The must-versus-may line is the trap.

question: Which of the following can NOT be issued as a participating policy?
explanation: Term (and Universal Life) are non-par only, with no bonus-sharing surplus. Whole Life, Endowment and anticipated endowment can be participating.

question: A married couple wants one policy that pays on the FIRST death, to clear their joint housing loan. This is a:
explanation: Two lives, pays on the first death, cover then ends: first-to-die joint life, the classic joint-mortgage structure. Last survivor pays on the second death.

question: Under a non-contributory (contractual) group scheme:
options: Employees pay part of the premium and may opt out
The employer pays all premiums and all eligible employees are automatically covered
The insurer requires a minimum participation rate
explanation: Non-contributory = employer pays everything, everyone eligible is auto-covered. Cost-sharing, opting out and minimum-participation belong to the voluntary/contributory plan.

question: Which is NOT a typical feature of group insurance compared with individual insurance?
options: One master contract covers many lives
Cover generally ceases when a member leaves the group
Each member selects their own sum assured and is individually underwritten
It is usually cheaper than equivalent individual cover
explanation: Group insurance uses group underwriting and employer-set cover, not individual selection and underwriting. The other three are genuine group features.

question: A grandmother takes out and owns an education endowment on her grandchild's life. This ownership type is:
explanation: Owner (grandmother) differs from the life insured (grandchild) with unequal roles: third-party. Single life would need owner and life insured to be the same person.

question: Since 1 October 2001, when selling group insurance products, a representative must:
options: Obtain MAS pre-approval for each policy
Offer a free-look period of 60 days
explanation: Since 1 Oct 2001 reps must complete a fact-find when selling group products. The other options are unrelated requirements.

question: A company insures a key executive whose value to the firm is expected to stay level for 10 years. Best policy and beneficiary:
explanation: Level need = level term; the company suffers the financial loss so it is the beneficiary. Key-person insurance is level term with the company as beneficiary.

question: Which statement about the renewable option on a term policy is FALSE?
options: Renewal requires no evidence of insurability
The premium rises to reflect attained age
The sum assured is reduced at each renewal
Renewals are usually allowed only up to about age 60
explanation: The sum assured is NOT reduced; renewal keeps the same benefit and term, only the premium rises. The other three are true. This is a NOT/FALSE question, mark each one.

question: Mdm Lee, 60, stops paying her whole life premiums. She wants cover to continue for the rest of her life with no further payments, accepting a smaller sum. The right non-forfeiture option is:
explanation: Lifelong cover, no further premiums, reduced amount = reduced paid-up (long-and-low). 'Lifelong' rules out extended term, which is a fixed period.

question: A policyholder wants to keep his FULL sum assured after stopping premiums, and doesn't mind that cover will run only for a fixed number of years. He should choose:
explanation: Full sum for a fixed period = extended term (tall-and-short). Reduced paid-up keeps lifetime cover but shrinks the sum. This is the mirror image of the reduced paid-up question.

question: Which is NOT a condition of converting a term policy to a permanent one?
options: A written request with the premium
New sum assured not exceeding the original
Original policy exclusions continue to apply
explanation: Evidence of insurability is NOT required, that waiver is the point of the option. The other three are genuine conditions.

question: A whole life policy accumulates a cash value primarily because:
options: The insurer adds an annual savings bonus
Level premiums exceed the true cost of risk in the early years, and the excess is invested
Policyholders pay a separate investment premium
MAS mandates a minimum cash value
explanation: Level premium on a rising risk means early overpayment, which is banked and invested: that is the cash value. It's structural, not an added feature.

question: An endowment policy is best suited to a client who wants:
options: A lump sum at a specific future date, plus protection until then
Lifelong cover with no savings element
Cover that decreases with a loan
explanation: Endowment matches a dated goal (education, retirement target) with protection meanwhile: maturity value on survival, sum assured on earlier death. Cheapest death-only = term; lifelong = whole life; loan-linked = decreasing term.

question: Mr Tan's anticipated endowment (sum assured S$40,000) has paid him S$12,000 in cashbacks. He dies before maturity. His beneficiaries receive:
explanation: Full S$40,000 sum assured. Cashbacks already received are not deducted from the death benefit.

question: When recommending a plain participating whole life policy (not bundled, not an ILP), which document is NOT required?
explanation: The Product Highlights Sheet is required only for ILPs. A plain par whole life needs the Cover Page, Product Summary and Policy Illustration (plus the LIA Guide).

question: On an online no-advice platform, a term policy is issued with a pre-existing-condition exclusion added at underwriting. Before the client can buy, the platform must:
options: Highlight the conditional acceptance and obtain the client's acknowledgement
Refer the client to a human adviser
explanation: Conditional acceptance (exclusions or loadings from underwriting) must be highlighted and acknowledged before purchase on a no-advice platform.
